sections.title: Let Us Count
sections.prose_summary: The chapter opens with real-life counting contexts — coconut trees arranged in a grid (top view), stacked cups, and laddoos and pedas arranged in trays. Students count objects by grouping rather than one-by-one, moving from concrete visual arrays to numerical answers. The section sits firmly at the concrete stage, using familiar objects and pictorial arrangements to build multiplicative counting intuition.

tasks.description: Count the coconut trees in Gundappa's land from the top-view grid. Write the number. How do you know?
Gundappa plucked 5 coconuts from each tree. How many coconuts has he plucked?
Count the number of cups Muniamma has made and write the number.
How many coconut laddoos are there in the trays?
How many milk pedas are there in the trays?

sections.title: Patterns with Money
sections.prose_summary: Students observe coins and notes arranged in visual patterns and count the total amounts. They then arrange play money (₹1, ₹2, ₹5, ₹10) to represent given amounts and ask peers to identify totals. The 'Two Ways' activity shows Shirley (groups of 3 and 5 coins) and Shiv (groups of 4 coins) — introducing the idea that the same quantity can be arranged differently and that arrangement reveals grouping structure. The model is concrete (physical play money) moving toward pictorial.

tasks.description: Look at Shirley's coin arrangement (circular pattern). How much money is it?
Look at Shiv's notes-and-coins grid arrangement. How much money is it?
How did you count them? Discuss in class.
Arrange play money of amounts 1, 2, 5, and 10 to show ₹36, ₹125, and ₹183. Ask your peers to tell how much it is.
Shirley and Shiv arranged coins in groups. Write the number of coins in the blank triangles for each arrangement.

sections.title: Even and Odd Numbers
sections.prose_summary: The pairing model (coins in pairs of 2) reveals even and odd numbers: Shiv's coins pair perfectly (even), Shirley's always have one left over (odd). Students describe each arrangement, list even and odd numbers from 1 to 20, and investigate whether times-2 numbers are always even. The section uses the concrete pairing act as a model, then moves to pictorial and symbolic classification.

tasks.book_ref: Two Ways / Even and Odd, p.36
tasks.description: Describe Shiv's arrangement and write his numbers.

tasks.book_ref: Two Ways / Even and Odd, p.36
tasks.description: Describe Shirley's arrangement and write her numbers.
Identify numbers between 1 and 20 as even or odd. You may draw the pairing arrangement.
Do you think all numbers in the times-2 table are even?

sections.title: Crayons Arrangement and Patterns
sections.prose_summary: A crayon-shaped number chart (1–50) with odd and even numbers in adjacent columns provides a visual pattern structure. Students classify given numbers as even or odd, discover the alternating page-number pattern, reason about neighbours of odd and even numbers, and explore consecutive number sequences. The section builds toward a generalisation about even/odd structure using multiple representations — chart, page numbers, digit-based rules — on the pictorial-to-symbolic path.

tasks.description: Circle the odd numbers and put a square around each even number from the given set (5, 8, 36, 69, 22, 51, 30, 38, 37, 52, 43). Use the crayons arrangement if needed.
Explore your textbook and find out what Shirley has seen. Draw a square on the even page numbers. Put a circle on the odd page numbers.
Shirley notices numbers before and after an odd number are even. Shiv wonders if numbers before and after an even number are odd. Check and discuss.
Choose any 10 consecutive numbers. Write whether they are even or odd below each number. What do you notice? Discuss.
Identify which of the following numbers are even and which are odd. Explain your reasoning: 30, 78, 300, 415, 46, 67, 154, 99.
Make two 2-digit numbers using digits 1 and 6 without repetition; identify as even or odd. Then choose any two digits and make 2-digit even numbers.
Are there more even or odd numbers between 1 and 100?
